Sean Stevenson, who's about this tall as a friend of mine, I always tell him he hangs out with me, so I make him feel less awkward. And when I was a kid, I was different than everyone. So I didn't really get along with other kids. I didn't have a lot of friends. I read a lot and I obsessed over what created success. How do we become successful? So I read about successful people and what I found, the more I read, was that they were all completely different. But I started to see patterns of how success was created. And when I became a consultant, I got up front and close and personal with those successful people. And the patterns are totally real. And I pulled their habits out and put them into this article. And those 10 habits that they employed that you probably don't, are exactly what creates success at a massive level. And you can take it and use it as a checklist. This individual is one of our online super fans and likes and watches everything that we do. And he watched two videos, one on how to walk in the morning, one on how to drink more water. And those two simple things gave him, after what appears to be a lifetime of trauma, where he had a father that was so high strung, he had several heart attacks and then died young. And this individual, every day, has to shower 15 to 20 times to feel okay in the world, because there's some obsessive compulsive behavior there. I was labeled slow, learning disabled, ADD. Sorry, I get emotional because my kid's here. Dyslexic, Asperger's Autistic, Retarded, and more by adults and physicians of authority. Because see, I don't think that anyone in this room really understands the difference between happy and sad. And I might sound crazy, like seriously, I'm the dude in the red glasses, so I can't. But what's the difference between sad, frustrated, pissed off, ticked off, confused, and I'm just plain not going to talk to you? Anybody want to take it? But every time they showed me that, I would think, is this a pop quiz I'm not prepared for? Because I didn't get it. Because people like us don't live for this. We live for this. When I say momentum, how many of you get a physiological response in your body, right? We live in three states and three states only. Forget what you've heard and stop pursuing something you'll never have. You want happiness, you're screwed. Because we don't get it. The only time we answer yes to are you happy is when we're in momentum and you know what I'm talking about. It's when things are going your way, when you're accomplishing what you want, when the world is following in your wake, when you're going up and you're going as fast as you can. How many of you know that's what it's all about, right? And let you tell me, when you're in momentum, are you physiologically actually stronger? Can you do more? When you're in momentum, do you think more clearly? Do you actually get more done? And here's the big one. When you're in momentum, do you feel that chemical shift in your body where you actually feel the high of momentum? How many of you know that's real? I point this out because you might show up the wrong day in an office where somebody has a thing called the prescription slip and be presenting as an entrepreneurial personality type in constraint, not in momentum, and someone might give you a pill. That the promise will give it to you and it'll never work. Because for people like us, there is one way we do this. We get into momentum. We figure out what it is we want. We figure out what we're going to do about it and how we're going to measure success. And we get up out of the water and start hydroplaning and make it happen and you all know what I mean. Because this is where we should be and there's a clear path to get there. Because the second state is when we're facing resistance. How many of you know what it's like to face resistance? Like the cards are stacked against you a little bit. It's really hard to get what you need to get done. But check this out. I want to just show you how crazy our personality type is. When we're facing resistance, here's what we do. We find this tiny little light at the end of the tunnel. And you can barely see it. And no one else thinks it's there. And then when they start to believe you it's there, they're like, it's a train. But you believe in it. And you move towards it. And you compel it towards you. And you step through it and that's where you create your life. How many of you know that? Because people like us, when we're facing resistance, we turn around and create momentum. It's who we are. That's how we transform. That's how we become who we are today and who we will be tomorrow. There's only one state we have to be careful with. It's when we're in constraint, when we're stuck in place, we can't see the light at the end of the tunnel. We don't know where we're going. How many of you know what that feels like? Yeah. How fast does your body break down? Almost immediately, right? So physiologically we become constrained. Cognitively, how many of you have felt the brain fog of constraint? You can't even think clearly. Your mind's racing or just completely shut down. And then chemically, when we go into constraint, we feel like we're standing in place for people like us that's unacceptable. We can't because we have to be in momentum. So it is your job to lower the pressure and noise in your life on a daily basis so that you can do more. What you heard Layla talk about to this morning was a way to become a transformational leader and lead a team that lowers the pressure and noise in your life. Because I want you all to understand something. Your team has one responsibility. One. Make your life easier. See, everybody thinks it's about profitability. But when your life gets easier, profitability happens. Because when your life gets easier, you get into momentum. When your life gets easier, you see things clearer. When you lower the pressure and noise in your life, that's when you find the momentum and you move forward and you don't experience constraint. How many of you know exactly what I'm talking about? How many of you are already right now as we sit here shifting perspective on how you look at your team? Good. Let's make it permanent because that's the way you should look at it. If you haven't yet, check out my momentum podcast. Because I share with you the answers to the questions that my highest level groups give me. I just record them on the podcast. They all listen to it first anyway. And I want you to build formidable teams. I want you to change what you're doing today so that you get more out of the team around you. Because I believe that every one of you is an entrepreneurial personality type and I want to very quickly just take you through four questions. See, I think there's four different types of people in the world. The first type is the caretakers, the people who want to take care of the people around them. Now, do we need this type of person in the world? We do. And a lot of entrepreneurs start thinking that they're this type of person when I bring this up. But I want to just ask you a simple question. Oh man, no slide. I just want to ask you a simple question. How many of you like to change bedpans? No seriously, nobody here, right? Because caretakers, like I've been in a room when someone has changed a bedpan and I said, did you enjoy doing that? And they'll say yes, if that person needed me, I'd feel fulfilled. I wanted to be here to do that. We need caretakers. It's just not you. I want you to qualify which one you are. The second type of person in the world, I call them the communicators. These are the ones that love to talk about what's going on. They love to share what's happening. They love to like tell people what's going on with other people. Now, a lot of entrepreneurs think they're communicators. How many of you enjoy small talk? Yeah. Are you just like me? Like as soon as small talks start you're like, you have two thoughts? How do I get out of here? Like, right? And do I fake a heart attack? So none of us are communicators. The third type of person in this world, do we need communicators? Guys, you need them in your companies. We needed them historically. Like evolutionarily, did we need the caretaker? Yes, because people like us never would have changed the bedpan and the human tribe would have stunk. Right? And did we need the communicators that talk to each other? Yes, but it's not us. I mean, you put two communicators at a water cooler. They'll talk for 45 minutes about a half hour TV show. I've seen it. I always feel like, you know those shows where you're watching the monkeys? I'm like, oh my God. Communicators in the wild. The third group is the coordinators. They're the ones that like, they love fine print, they love contracts. Not because you're going to cut a deal, but because you're going to write a contract. They like like the rules and regulations. Anybody, a communicator here is the question. How many of you enjoy being on a committee? So there's only one type of person left. And the qualifying question for this one is, can you turn it off? Is there anyone who can? Then the whole room qualifies for the fourth group of human being. And if we look at that evolutionary tribe that I just spoke of, and we had the people who take care of each other, who communicate, who coordinate, what are we missing? Evolutionary hunters. And it's my sincere belief that everyone in this room, who's felt isolated and different and alone, and sorry, like you were a party of one, I just totally gave him an elbow to the back of the head. That sucks. Everyone of you is part of the most important tribe in history. Because we are that small percentage of the population that gets up every morning, goes into the future, discovers a new reality, comes back to the present, and insists it become real. But it's why we feel so comfortable right here. Because this is our tribe. And it starts as this whisper that says, hey, you can do more, you can be more, you can leave more behind. But then it gets louder and louder, and then it's the only voice you hear. How many of you know exactly what I'm talking about? Here's the challenge for people like us, is that our infrastructure falls woefully short of the contribution we want to make. How many of you know that feeling of feeling like you can't go fast enough? I hate that feeling. I drive race cars on the street, I hate going slow. I don't care if I can only go 60 miles an hour, I'm going to get there really, really fast. How many of you know what I'm talking about? So when the infrastructure is not there, that's where we get stuck. I've worked with products that didn't exist and became billion-dollar companies. And we weren't anywhere near what Alex and Layla are doing. Those that are here and those who aren't. And so congratulations. I'm going to go through this quickly because this is my content. So I'm waiting for those until I do run the billion-dollar company. So we're going up to a hundred million today. Is that okay with everybody? But I want you to understand what's going to follow directly after me. I'm going through this fast. You can take notes if you want to. We'll give you a link where you can get a download of this. But directly after this, we're going to share the Jim billionaire code. Because, yeah. So as I take you through this, I just want you to be thinking to yourself, every time you think, how am I going to apply this? I just want you to know it's already figured out. Like that reduction in pressure and noise has been Alex and I spending, probably collectively, about 25 or 30 hours together independently and together to create that one. But I want to give you this one as an introduction. How many of you want me to start now? All right. So there's a level off the billionaire code. It's when you're in the crowd. I discussed the crowd earlier. How many of you are familiar with that feeling? You know what I'm talking about, right? My daughter and I went to the rodeo and said, two daughters, we took them to go see a band that they like. It was like a classical music band. My kids are so weird. And 22,000 people at the rodeo, I'd never felt so alone in my life. How many of you know that feeling, right? It's like, oh man, I'm with the drones. And so now you get off of the out of the crowd by becoming a seeker. A seeker is that early entrepreneur, a seeker's annual revenue. Oh man, we're going to cut off a little bit, but that's okay. I'm going to keep going. A seeker's annual revenue is zero to 40,000 in the United States. That looks like it says 6 million. There's 16 million businesses, zero to $40,000. Here's what the seeker's going through. Your time when you're a seeker, you're dabbling. How many of you remember this? Like you were thinking about doing it, kind of not doing it, thinking about doing it. Like how many of you accidentally got into business? Remember that? Yeah, I see. A lot of seekers. Your primary focus is self identity. The question that we all ask at this level is like, how do I stop the pressure and noise? Because when you're a seeker, everything looks like an opportunity. Everything looks too hard. It's confusing. You guys remember going through this. Your team is you got to pick a team. Your biggest need at this level is keystone habits, routine, daily planning, setting outcomes, and personal care. One thing I do want you to know is anytime I get to that bottom thing that says biggest need, you don't have it covered by a process, a system, a person, or a project. You got to put one in place. Because as you ascend the billionaire code, you don't stop doing this stuff. You just got to do something different and have it covered. To graduate, you understand who you want to serve. The transition from seeker to the next level of the billionaire code apprentice. Oh man, I think we call this starter now. Good. So starter now, annual revenue of starters is 40 to $100,000. There's six million starters in the United States. There's 22 million total under $100,000 companies in the United States out of 29 million. How many of you are over $100,000? Okay, you beat 22 million. Doesn't that give you a reality check on business? The starters, time is part time. You're not usually fully committed at this point. The primary focus is market identity. Who am I going to sell to? The fallback question is what is wrong with me? Remember those early days, like you always feel broken, like it's you, right? Like why won't they do this with me?
